content.text: Incident Action Backlog Governance

Generating action items from postmortems is the easy part. Keeping those items moving from open to closed — without losing context, duplicating work, or quietly retiring items that were never completed — requires an explicit governance model. Backlog governance for incident action items is distinct from ordinary sprint backlog management: the items carry safety, reliability, and customer trust implications that ordinary feature work does not.
What Backlog Governance Is and Why It Differs
Standard sprint backlog governance prioritises feature delivery velocity. Incident action item governance prioritises
system reliability improvement velocity
. The two are not the same.

Ordinary backlog items can be indefinitely deferred with low cost. An action item from a SEV-1 that addressed a model safety failure cannot be deferred indefinitely — every day it stays open is a day the system is potentially re-exposed to the same failure. Governance must reflect this difference.
Three structural properties separate incident action backlogs from feature backlogs:
Time-bound by SLA
— items carry external deadlines set by severity, not by product priority
Cross-team by nature
— many items require coordination across ML, platform, and product teams that do not share a sprint board
Safety-weighted
— items from safety-relevant incidents carry implicit deployment gates that feature work does not
The Triage Gate

Every action item passes through a weekly triage gate before entering the active backlog. The triage gate is a 30-minute weekly meeting (or async equivalent) run by the postmortem owner or on-call lead. Its three outputs for each item are:
Owner confirmed
(named individual, not a team)
SLA due date set
(from the postmortem publication date, not the meeting date)
Priority score computed
(see formula in the diagram above)

Items that cannot be triaged in a single cycle — because the root cause is still unclear or the fix approach is contested — stay in a 'Needs Definition' holding state, not in the active backlog. A 'Needs Definition' item that persists for more than 5 business days triggers an immediate synchronous discussion between the postmortem owner and the relevant engineering leads.
Priority Scoring
Rather than relying on subjective judgement, use a numeric priority score to order the backlog. The formula:

def priority_score(item):
    sev_weights = {"SEV-1": 10, "SEV-2": 6, "SEV-3": 2}
    sev_score = sev_weights.get(item["severity"], 1) * 10

    # How many postmortems referenced this same root cause?
    recurrence_score = item.get("recurrence_count", 0) * 5

    # Urgency increases as SLA deadline approaches
    days_until_breach = (item["sla_due_date"] - today()).days
    urgency_score = max(0, (14 - days_until_breach) * 2)

    # Safety-relevant items get a fixed bonus
    safety_bonus = 20 if item.get("safety_tagged") else 0

    return sev_score + recurrence_score + urgency_score + safety_bonus

This scoring ensures that a recurring SEV-2 (same root cause in three incidents) outranks a one-off SEV-1 with days remaining on its SLA, because recurrence is a leading indicator of future large-scale failure.
Backlog Health Zones
A governance model without a health signal is just process theatre. Define three health zones that the team reviews monthly:
Green (Healthy)
SLA compliance rate > 90% over the rolling 90 days
No single item older than 1.5x its SLA (e.g., no SEV-2 open past 10 business days)
Open item count is less than twice the team's sprint velocity for reliability work
Deferral rate below 15%
Yellow (Watch)
SLA compliance rate between 75–90%
One or two items between 1.5x and 2x their SLA age
Open item count between 2x and 3x sprint velocity
Deferral rate 15–30%

Yellow triggers a governance review: the engineering manager meets with the postmortem owner to identify whether the backlog is too large, items are too coarsely scoped, or the team simply lacks capacity.
Red (Intervention Required)
SLA compliance rate below 75%
Any item older than 2x its SLA
Open item count exceeding 3x sprint velocity
A recurring root cause that has appeared in postmortems three or more times without a completed action item

Red state is escalated to VP Engineering. It signals one of three systemic problems: too many incidents generating too many items, insufficient engineering capacity allocated to reliability work, or a governance process that is being ignored.
Sprint Integration Patterns

The most common failure in incident action backlog governance is treating the backlog as separate from the sprint. Engineers have two to-do lists — sprint board and incident items — and the sprint board consistently wins because it is what the team is measured on.
Fix this by integrating incident action items into the standard sprint process:
Reliability Budget Allocation
Allocate a fixed percentage of sprint capacity to reliability work (incident action items included). A typical allocation for AI platform teams:
Team Stability
Recommended Reliability Budget
Rationale
High incident volume (> 3 SEV-2/month)
30% of sprint capacity
Active reliability debt requires sustained investment
Moderate (1-3 SEV-2/month)
20% of sprint capacity
Standard operational health maintenance
Low (< 1 SEV-2/month)
10% of sprint capacity
Maintenance mode; respond to SEV-1 items immediately
This budget is non-negotiable for items within their SLA window. Feature work is deferred before incident action items breach SLA.
Sprint Planning Gate
At the start of every sprint planning session, run a one-minute check before reviewing feature tickets:

Sprint Planning: Incident Action Item Gate (< 5 minutes)

1. Pull all items where SLA_due_date falls within this sprint or has passed.
2. These are MUST-DO items -- they enter the sprint before any feature ticket.
3. For each: confirm owner is present in the sprint and has capacity.
4. Items that cannot fit must trigger an escalation, not a quiet deferral.
This makes SLA-approaching items visible to the whole team at the moment capacity decisions are being made, rather than invisible until they breach.
Cross-Team Governance
Many incident action items require work from teams that share neither a sprint board nor a reporting chain. These are the most common source of SLA breaches.
The Governance Contact Pattern
For each cross-team action item, designate one person in each involved team:
Driver
: the named owner, typically from the team that ran the postmortem
Liaison
: a counterpart in the dependent team, agreed at triage

The driver-liaison pair runs a dedicated sync at least once per week for SEV-1 items and once per sprint cycle for SEV-2 items. The driver reports status in the incident backlog; the liaison ensures the dependent team's work is sprint-scheduled.
Dependency Tagging
In the issue tracker, tag cross-team items with a structured dependency label:

labels:
  - postmortem-action
  - sev-2
  - depends:ml-platform    # team this item is blocked on
  - driver:ai-serving      # team responsible for closing

# Automated query: all items blocked on a specific team
gh issue list --label "depends:ml-platform" --label "postmortem-action" --state open

This makes it trivial to generate a per-team dependency report: each team can query which active incident items need their work, without having to attend the postmortem review meetings of other teams.
Handling Backlog Debt

Most AI platform teams inherit a backlog with items that are months old, unowned, and poorly specified. Rather than writing off these items or letting them persist indefinitely, apply a structured amnesty process:
Quarterly Amnesty Review
Pull all items older than 1.5x their original SLA

For each item, make a binary decision within 15 minutes: still relevant? reschedule with new owner + due date. Root cause resolved by other work? close with link to resolution. No longer a realistic risk? formally defer to watch list with a 90-day reminder.
Document every decision in the issue. 'Closed because X' and 'Deferred because Y' are valid — silent removal is not.

Amnesty is not the same as abandonment. Every item that is formally deferred or closed must have a documented reason. An item that is closed without evidence of resolution should be treated with the same seriousness as a false-positive alarm: it means the team believes the risk is gone when it may not be.
Governance for AI-Specific Item Categories
AI platform postmortems tend to generate item categories that do not map cleanly to the traditional software engineering backlog:
Evaluation Harness Items
Items that require building or extending model evaluation coverage (e.g., 'add red-teaming coverage for prompt injection after the content safety bypass incident') have a different lifecycle:
Implementation requires dataset curation, eval design, and model iteration — not just a code change
Closure criteria must include a measured evaluation score, not just 'eval added'
These items commonly take 2–4 weeks even for SEV-2 incidents. Define a milestone schedule at triage rather than a single SLA date
Infrastructure Capacity Items
After a capacity-exhaustion incident, action items often involve infrastructure scaling changes (reserved GPU capacity, auto-scaling limits, multi-region failover). These items:
Require finance approval for spend above a threshold — build approval lead time into the SLA
May need to be provisioned in stages to avoid budget shocks — specify milestones in closure criteria
Must be verified under realistic load, not just checked at provisioning time
Model Retrain Items

When an incident reveals that a model was trained on corrupted or out-of-distribution data, the action item involves a full retrain cycle. Governance for these items uses a milestone-gated closure model:

Model Retrain Action Item -- Milestone Closure Model

Milestone 1 (Day 3):  Corrected dataset identified and validated
Milestone 2 (Day 7):  Training run initiated on corrected data
Milestone 3 (Day 14): Evaluation suite passed on new model checkpoint
Milestone 4 (Day 17): Staged rollout to 5% traffic
Milestone 5 (Day 21): Full rollout, item closed

Each milestone is updated in the issue tracker by the driver.
Failure to reach a milestone on schedule triggers escalation, not SLA extension.
Tooling Integration

Effective backlog governance depends on the backlog being a single source of truth — not a spreadsheet, not a Confluence page, not a Slack pinned message. Use your existing issue tracker with structured labels and a small amount of automation:

# Weekly backlog health report (runs as a GitHub Actions cron job)
import datetime
from github import Github
from collections import defaultdict

def generate_backlog_health_report(repo_name, token):
    g = Github(token)
    repo = g.get_repo(repo_name)

    issues = repo.get_issues(labels=["postmortem-action"], state="open")
    today = datetime.date.today()

    sla_map = {"sev-1": 3, "sev-2": 7, "sev-3": 30}
    zones = {"green": [], "yellow": [], "red": []}
    total_closed_90d = 0  # would query closed issues separately

    for issue in issues:
        sev = next(
            (l.name for l in issue.labels if l.name.startswith("sev-")), None
        )
        if not sev:
            continue

        sla_days = sla_map.get(sev, 30)
        # SLA due date from custom field or label (simplified: use created_at)
        sla_due = issue.created_at.date() + datetime.timedelta(days=sla_days)
        days_over = (today - sla_due).days

        if days_over > sla_days * 1.0:      # > 2x SLA
            zones["red"].append(issue)
        elif days_over > sla_days * 0.5:    # > 1.5x SLA
            zones["yellow"].append(issue)
        else:
            zones["green"].append(issue)

    return {
        "green_count": len(zones["green"]),
        "yellow_count": len(zones["yellow"]),
        "red_count": len(zones["red"]),
        "red_items": [
            {"title": i.title, "assignee": i.assignee.login if i.assignee else "unassigned"}
            for i in zones["red"]
        ],
    }
Summary

Incident action backlog governance provides the operational structure that turns postmortem commitments into verified system improvements. The core components are a weekly triage gate that assigns owners and SLA dates, a priority scoring formula that surfaces high-risk items, health zones (green/yellow/red) that trigger proportional responses, and sprint integration that makes reliability work non-negotiable before feature work. Cross-team items require designated driver-liaison pairs and dependency tagging. Quarterly amnesty reviews clear backlog debt without silently abandoning items. For AI platforms, evaluation harness, infrastructure capacity, and model retrain items each require modified closure models that reflect their longer, milestone-based delivery cycles.
Key takeaways:
Triage every item within one week: assign owner, SLA date, and priority score
Health zones (green/yellow/red) make backlog state visible and trigger proportional escalation
Reserve a fixed sprint budget for reliability work before feature tickets are pulled
Cross-team items need a driver and a liaison -- not a shared ticket and hope
Quarterly amnesty turns stale backlog debt into explicit decisions rather than quiet rot
